data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "No fixed (B, phi, psi, k) regime is sufficient to predict strict harness advantage; harness strictly beats self-consistency only when the critic's error profile is question-conditionally aligned such that it flags wrong candidates on questions where the majority is wrong (beneficial filtering) and does not flag right candidates on questions where the majority is right (harmful filtering). The advantage is determined by the conditional probability P(critic flags majority-wrong question | majority wrong) vs P(critic flags majority-right question | majority right), not by marginal precision/recall.","mechanism": "Marginal precision and recall average over all questions, but the harness outcome depends on the joint distribution of critic flags and majority correctness at the question level. Two critics with identical marginal (phi, psi) can produce opposite harness effects: Critic A flags wrong candidates only on already-majority-wrong questions (beneficial, never harmful) while Critic B flags wrong candidates uniformly at random across question types (harmful false flags on majority-right questions cancel beneficial true flags). The observed data confirms this: even with recall=1.0 on the hard v4-pro set, precision=0.125 meant the critic flagged many correct candidates on majority-right questions, yielding net zero. The strict-advantage condition is therefore: P(flag on majority-wrong) > P(flag on majority-right) by a margin exceeding the tie-creation rate.","scope": {"covers": ["any k >= 3", "any base error rate", "critics with measurable question-level flag rates", "both independent and correlated candidate errors"], "excludes": ["critics whose flag decisions are identical across questions (trivially uniform)", "k=1 (no majority to filter)"]},"forbidden": [{"case": "Two critic configurations with identical marginal (phi, psi) but different question-conditional flag rates produce harness-vs-SC deltas within 0.02 of each other (would refute the question-conditional primacy claim)", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "A critic with marginal phi=0.7, psi=0.7 at B=0.3, k=5 but uniform question-level flag rates produces harness improvement > 0.03 over SC (would refute: marginal stats are insufficient)", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "A critic with marginal phi=0.5, psi=0.5 but question-conditional alignment P(flag|majority-wrong) > 0.8, P(flag|majority-right) < 0.2 does NOT produce strict harness improvement (would refute the alignment sufficiency claim)", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "The measured question-conditional flag rate difference does not predict harness-vs-SC delta with R-squared > 0.4 across the 12 cells (would refute predictive power of alignment metric)", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}],"prose_notes": "This conjecture subsumes and generalizes the marginal-regime prediction. It predicts that even in a 'favorable' marginal regime, a uniform-flag critic will fail, while a question-adaptive critic with worse marginal stats can succeed. The experiment uses three critic variants to decouple marginal from conditional alignment: one question-aware critic, one question-blind critic, and one adversarial-flag critic that deliberately flags majority-right questions."}
